title: Daily Interview English
description: Think-aloud system design walkthroughs, estimation practice, mock interviews, and English patterns for tech professionals preparing for interviews.
author: Daily Interview English

episodes.title: The Way I See It — 4-Second Pause Protocol
episodes.description: The very first episode. Today's pattern: 'The way I see it is...' for stating opinions without sounding defensive. Plus: the 4-Second Pause Protocol for unexpected interview questions.
episodes.pattern: The way I see it is...

episodes.title: What clicked for me was... — 4-Second Pause Protocol
episodes.description: Welcome to Daily Interview English. Episode 2, July 05, 2026.  Today's episode draws from Changelog: "From open source hits to OpenAI (Interview)".  Today's pattern: "What clicked for me was..."  Use this to explain a key realization or motivation. It's more natural than 'I realized' and signals genuine insight.  Here are three examples. Repeat each one out loud after me.  Example 1. What clicked for me was realizing that coding agents let me build software tailored to a team's exact workflow.

episodes.title: The way I see it is... — STAR-LA Framework
episodes.description: Welcome to Daily Interview English. Episode 3, July 05, 2026.  Today's episode draws from Changelog: "From open source hits to OpenAI (Interview)".  Today's pattern: "The way I see it is..."  States an opinion without sounding rigid. Signals 'this is my view but I'm open to discussion.'  Here are three examples. Repeat each one out loud after me.  Example 1. The way I see it is, we should optimize for latency first and add caching layers later.  Example 2. The way I see it is, this trade-off fav
episodes.pattern: The way I see it is...

episodes.title: That said... — Three Ways to Say I Don't Know
episodes.description: Welcome to Daily Interview English. Episode 4, July 05, 2026.  Today's pattern: "That said..."  Introduces a counterpoint or qualification. More natural than 'however' in speech.  Here are three examples. Repeat each one out loud after me.  Example 1. That said, the trade-off was worth it given the timeline we were working with.  Example 2. That said, I'd do the migration differently if I were starting today.  Example 3. That said, the approach worked well for our scale and I'd recommend it for
episodes.tip: Three Ways to Say I Don't Know

episodes.title: What I'd really highlight is... — 4-Second Pause Protocol
episodes.description: Welcome to Daily Interview English. Episode 5, July 05, 2026.  Today's pattern: "What I'd really highlight is..."  Directs attention to the most important part. Shows you know what matters and what's detail.  Here are three examples. Repeat each one out loud after me.  Example 1. What I'd really highlight is that the adoption was organic — nobody mandated these tools.  Example 2. What I'd really highlight is how the team's velocity changed after we rebuilt the workflow.  Example 3. What I'd real

episodes.title: To put a finer point on it... — 4-Second Pause Protocol
episodes.description: Welcome to Daily Interview English. Episode 6, July 05, 2026.  Today's pattern: "To put a finer point on it..."  Refines a previous statement to be more precise or impactful. Shows you're thinking critically about your own words.  Here are three examples. Repeat each one out loud after me.  Example 1. To put a finer point on it — we didn't just speed up triage. We eliminated the need for manual triage entirely.  Example 2. To put a finer point on it — the real innovation wasn't the AI. It was re
episodes.pattern: To put a finer point on it...

episodes.title: I haven't worked directly with that. What I can tell you is... — 4-Second Pause Protocol
episodes.description: Welcome to Daily Interview English. Episode 7, July 05, 2026.  Today's pattern: "I haven't worked directly with that. What I can tell you is..."  Honest about gaps, confident about adjacent experience. Transforms a weakness into a demonstration of learning ability.  Here are three examples. Repeat each one out loud after me.  Example 1. I haven't worked directly with Kubernetes at that scale. What I can tell you is that I've designed distributed systems handling 50K requests per second, and the
episodes.pattern: I haven't worked directly with that. What I can tell you is...

episodes.title: This resulted in... — 4-Second Pause Protocol
episodes.description: Welcome to Daily Interview English. Episode 8, July 05, 2026.  Today's pattern: "This resulted in..."  Simple, direct cause-to-effect. Always follow with a number or specific outcome.  Here are three examples. Repeat each one out loud after me.  Example 1. This resulted in the CVE workload dropping by 80 percent within the first month.  Example 2. This resulted in the BA team adopting the agents without any formal rollout — they just started using them.  Example 3. This resulted in us shipping t

episodes.title: Self-Introduction Practice — Present-Past-Future
episodes.description: Special practice episode: your self-introduction using the Present-Past-Future framework. Covers trading systems experience, legacy migration, early coding agent adoption, vulnerability platform, security tool ($100K+ subscription replacement), internal dev platform, outside interests, and the interview close. Includes section-by-section breakdown, memory anchors, and a 60-second backup.

episodes.title: Prepared Q&A — 7 Key Interview Questions
episodes.description: Special practice episode: prepared answers for 7 key interview questions. Tell me about yourself, Why this company, Why leaving current role, Built something from zero, Worked with non-engineers, Security approach, What you're looking for. Each uses STAR-LA or Structure-First with real stories. Includes key numbers quick reference.

episodes.title: SD Fundamentals Think-Aloud — CAP, ACID vs BASE, Consensus
episodes.description: Welcome to System Design Think-Aloud. Today we're working through three fundamental concepts: CAP theorem, ACID versus BASE, and consensus protocols. These come up in almost every system design interview, not as direct questions, but as the vocabulary you need when discussing trade-offs.  I'm going to reason through these out loud, exactly like I would in an actual interview. Listen for how I connect concepts to real systems and name trade-offs explicitly.  Let's start with CAP theorem.  OK, so

episodes.title: Infrastructure Think-Aloud — Load Balancing, Caching, CDN, Message Queues
episodes.description: Welcome to System Design Think-Aloud. Today we're working through four infrastructure building blocks: load balancing, caching, content delivery networks, and message queues. These are the components you'll reference in almost every system design interview.  Let me reason through each one out loud, focusing on the trade-offs and when to pick which option.  Let's start with load balancing.  OK, so load balancing is about distributing incoming traffic across multiple servers. The first question I'

episodes.title: Data Architecture Think-Aloud — Sharding, Replication, Partitioning
episodes.description: Welcome to System Design Think-Aloud. Today we're working through data architecture: sharding, replication, and consistent hashing. These are the techniques you use when your data is too big for a single machine, which in an interview, it almost always is.  Let me reason through each one, focusing on the trade-offs and the failure scenarios.  Let's start with database sharding.  So, sharding is about horizontally partitioning your data across multiple database instances. Each shard holds a subse

episodes.title: API & Microservices Think-Aloud — API Design, Microservices Architecture
episodes.description: Welcome to System Design Think-Aloud. Today we're working through API design and microservices architecture. These come up in almost every system design interview because they define how your system's components communicate and how you decompose a large system into manageable pieces.  Let's start with API design.  So, the three main API styles are REST, gRPC, and GraphQL. Let me think through when to use each.  REST is the default choice. It's resource-oriented, uses HTTP verbs, and is stateless

episodes.title: Reliability Think-Aloud — Idempotency, Rate Limiting, Observability
episodes.description: Welcome to System Design Think-Aloud. Today we're working through three reliability topics: idempotency, rate limiting, and observability. These are what separate systems that work in demos from systems that work in production.  Let me reason through each one, focusing on the failure scenarios and how to handle them.  Let's start with idempotency.  So, an operation is idempotent if executing it multiple times has the same effect as executing it once. This sounds abstract, but it's critical in di

episodes.title: URL Shortener Think-Aloud — Full walkthrough with estimation
episodes.description: Welcome to System Design Think-Aloud. Today we're doing a full walkthrough of a classic interview problem: design a URL shortener like Bitly.  I'm going to work through this exactly like I would in an interview. Clarify requirements, estimate scale, design the high-level architecture, dive deep into the key component, and name the trade-offs.  Let's get started.  So the problem is: design a URL shortener. The first thing I'd do is clarify the requirements.  What are the functional requirements?

episodes.title: Chat System Think-Aloud — Real-time messaging, WebSocket vs polling
episodes.description: Welcome to System Design Think-Aloud. Today we're working through another classic: design a chat system like WhatsApp or Slack.  Let me work through this step by step, exactly like in an interview.  So, the problem is: design a real-time messaging system. First, let me clarify the requirements. We need to support one-on-one chats and group chats. Users need to see when their contacts are online. Messages should be stored so you can see history when you log back in. And we need to handle push not

episodes.title: News Feed Think-Aloud — Fan-out, caching, ranking
episodes.description: Welcome to System Design Think-Aloud. Today we're working through design a news feed, the Instagram or Twitter problem. This is one of the most common system design interview questions because it touches on so many concepts: fan-out strategies, caching, ranking, and real-time updates.  Let me work through this step by step.  So, the problem is: generate a personalized feed of posts from people you follow. Users can post, follow other users, and scroll through their feed. The feed should be ranke

episodes.title: Payment System Think-Aloud — Fintech: idempotency, double-charge, reconciliation
episodes.description: Welcome to System Design Think-Aloud. Today we're designing a payment system, which is one of the most important fintech system design questions. If you're interviewing for a fintech company, this WILL come up.  I'm going to work through this as if I'm in an actual interview, focusing on the things that make payment systems different from other systems: financial correctness, idempotency, reconciliation, and regulatory compliance.  Let's start by clarifying the requirements. A payment system nee

episodes.title: Ledger System Think-Aloud — Fintech: double-entry, append-only, ACID, audit
episodes.description: Welcome to System Design Think-Aloud. Today we're designing a ledger system, the double-entry accounting system that records every financial transaction. This is the source of truth for account balances in any fintech company.  Let me reason through this step by step.  So, what is a ledger? At its core, a ledger records every financial movement as a pair of entries. Every transaction has a debit entry and a credit entry, and they must always balance to zero. If I transfer a hundred dollars from

episodes.title: Trading Platform Think-Aloud — Fintech: order matching, real-time feeds, FIFO
episodes.description: Welcome to System Design Think-Aloud. Today we're designing a trading platform, like a stock exchange. This is one of the hardest system design problems because of the extreme latency requirements. We're talking sub-millisecond matching, which means the architecture is fundamentally different from typical web systems.  Let me work through this step by step.  So, a trading platform matches buy and sell orders. The core component is the order book. For each instrument, like a stock, there's a list

episodes.title: Fraud Detection Think-Aloud — Fintech: stream processing, feature stores, rules vs ML
episodes.description: Welcome to System Design Think-Aloud. Today we're designing a fraud detection system. This is a fascinating problem because it's at the intersection of real-time stream processing, machine learning, and human operations. And it has one of the most interesting trade-offs in all of system design: catching more fraud versus creating more false positives that annoy legitimate customers.  Let me work through this step by step.  So, the problem is: detect fraudulent transactions in real-time. When a t

episodes.title: System Design Interviews — The Complete Framework
episodes.description: Sriram Panyam (ex-Google, ex-LinkedIn) breaks down the system design interview: 5-phase approach, 25 canonical problems, what interviewers look for at each level, and the key mindset shift from buzzword-driven to requirement-driven design. The single best SDI resource.

episodes.title: Event Sourcing & CQRS — Architecture Deep Dive
episodes.description: Martin Dilger (author of Understanding Event Sourcing) explains event stores, projections, CQRS, event versioning, and why Kafka is NOT an event store. Practical adoption strategy: 2-day modeling workshop + 1-day build.

episodes.title: Rate Limiting & High-Traffic Systems — Real Incident Walkthrough
episodes.description: Mojtaba Sarooghi (Queue-it) on handling flash traffic: 100K to 100M requests in one minute, cold-start failures, DynamoDB at 200K TPS, rate limiting algorithms, and bot detection (98% of traffic is bots during ticket drops).

episodes.title: Message Queues & Event-Driven Architecture — Patterns & Tradeoffs
episodes.description: Chris Patterson (MassTransit creator) on message brokers vs event streaming, sagas vs routing slips, transactional outbox pattern, dead-letter queues. Transport-agnostic architectural patterns for distributed systems.

episodes.title: Consistent Hashing Explained (ByteByteGo)
episodes.description: Visual explainer on consistent hashing — how distributed systems assign keys to nodes and minimize redistribution when nodes are added or removed. Core concept for sharding and distributed caches.

episodes.title: CAP Theorem Simplified (ByteByteGo)
episodes.description: Why distributed systems must choose between Consistency, Availability, and Partition Tolerance. Clear explanation with real-world database examples (CP vs AP systems).

episodes.title: Rate Limiter Design — Token Bucket & Leaky Bucket (ByteByteGo)
episodes.description: Token bucket, leaky bucket, fixed window, sliding window — the four rate limiting algorithms explained. How to scale rate limiters in a distributed system.

episodes.title: How a URL Shortener Works — System Design Walkthrough (ByteByteGo)
episodes.description: End-to-end system design for a URL shortener: hash functions, base62 encoding, database schema, caching strategy, and scaling to millions of URLs.

episodes.title: API Gateway Explained (ByteByteGo)
episodes.description: What an API gateway does: authentication, rate limiting, request routing, protocol translation, and service discovery. How it fits into a microservices architecture.

episodes.title: Estimation Practice I — Twitter QPS, Google storage, WhatsApp bandwidth
episodes.description: Welcome to Estimation Practice. Today we're working through 3 back-of-envelope estimation problems.  The key to estimation is being explicit about your assumptions, showing your math step by step, and sanity-checking your answer. Let's practice.  Problem 1: Twitter QPS and Storage.  So the question is: Estimate the queries per second and storage for Twitter.  Let me start by stating my assumptions.  Assumption 1: 1.5 billion total users  Assumption 2: 300 million daily active users  Assumption 3

episodes.title: Estimation Practice II — Payment TPS, Netflix CDN, Uber location writes
episodes.description: Welcome to Estimation Practice. Today we're working through 3 back-of-envelope estimation problems.  The key to estimation is being explicit about your assumptions, showing your math step by step, and sanity-checking your answer. Let's practice.  Problem 1: Payment System TPS.  So the question is: Estimate transactions per second for a global payment processor like Stripe.  Let me start by stating my assumptions.  Assumption 1: Processes payments for 1 million businesses  Assumption 2: Average b
